--- SOURCE 1: Effective Professional Development for Educators: Strategies for ... ---
URL: https://onlineprograms.education.uiowa.edu/blog/effective-professional-development-for-educators

SUMMARY:
Teacher professional development includes a variety of formats, such as workshops, conferences, online courses, and peer collaboration.

FULL CONTENT:
![The University of Iowa](https://assets.everspringpartners.com/87/63/7ffb08fe46bf8d9220eb7cf5a1f7/iowa-logo.svg)

# Effective Professional Development for Educators: Strategies for Success

![professor in polka dot top lecturing a class in professional development](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNTJweCIgd2lkdGg9IjgwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=)

Professional development is essential for helping teachers and education professionals maintain up-to-date skills and knowledge needed for effective teaching. An evolving educational landscape, including demographic changes and policy shifts, and new, research-based knowledge on effective practices, require educators to commit to continuous learning.1

This ongoing learning can support career advancement while fostering student success through more effective instruction. Explore the types of professional development teachers can undertake, and current information about effective teaching strategies.

## Professional Development for Better Teaching Strategies

Professional development helps educators keep current with educational trends and improve teaching strategies, ultimately boosting student engagement. Schools and districts often sponsor these opportunities, providing the necessary time and resources for teachers to enhance their skills. A recent study co-authored by University of Iowa faculty member Pamela Wesely emphasizes the role of conferences in professional development for language teachers. The study found that such conferences not only offer active learning experiences but also foster a sense of community and collaboration among teachers, supporting pedagogical innovation and professional community.2

Well-designed professional development programs can offer teachers opportunities to collaborate with peers in addressing topics relevant to their classrooms and adapt to new challenges, leading to better student learning outcomes.3

### Professional Development and Teacher Training Programs

Teacher professional development includes a variety of formats, such as workshops, conferences, online courses, and peer collaboration. Workshops can range from a couple of hours to a full day of intensive sessions aimed at enhancing instructional strategies, and conferences allow teachers to learn from experts and peers outside their usual environment. Online courses offer flexible learning opportunities that teachers can fit into their schedules, while peer collaboration often occurs in professional learning communities, where educators share expertise and plan together.

Topics in professional development programs might cover subjects ranging from lesson planning and assessment strategies to classroom management strategies and curriculum adaptation, instructional coaching, and subject-specific techniques, providing teachers with various professional growth opportunities.3

Comprehensive teacher training programs aim to ensure teachers are well-equipped to adapt to the dynamic educational environment. They typically blend theory and practice, emphasizing pedagogy, content knowledge, and hands-on experiences such as student teaching or mentorship, to prepare educators to meet classroom challenges.

## What Makes Professional Development Effective?

Research has shown that several elements contribute to the effectiveness of professional development for educators. These components help teachers refine their instructional strategies, promoting student success.4

Mentorship and coaching play crucial roles in providing personalized support to teachers. Coaches and mentors offer feedback and guidance tailored to individual needs, helping teachers apply new strategies effectively in the classroom.4

Practical, hands-on experiences in job-embedded contexts are vital in teacher training programs, allowing educators to practice and implement new skills within their classroom settings. Teachers gain confidence in applying innovative teaching methods by participating in activities that mirror real classroom scenarios.4

Models and modeling effective practices offer teachers clear examples of best practices. This can include lesson plans, sample student work, and video or written cases, giving teachers a vision of effective strategies to implement in their classrooms.4

Other key elements of effective professional development include:4

Integrating these elements into professional development opportunities helps create meaningful improvement in teaching practices, leading to increased student progress.

## Adapting Teaching Practices for Evolving Student Learning Needs

Today's teachers find the profession through various pathways, including traditional university-based programs, alternative certification routes, and registered apprenticeships.5 Regardless of the path into teaching, ongoing professional development and continuing education help education professionals adapt to the ever-changing and complex realities of the classroom.

Continuous improvement of instructional practices, achieved through feedback, reflection, and professional development, ensures educators remain effective and responsive to evolving skills that students need. These 21st-century competencies include critical thinking, complex problem-solving, mastering challenging content, effective communication and collaboration, and self-direction.4

## Continuing Education Pathways

Educators can pursue advanced degrees, certifications, and specialized training programs to enhance their skills. Options include master's programs, National Board Certification, and curriculum-based professional development, which provide tailored learning experiences to meet diverse educational needs.3

## Continuing Education Supports Career Advancement

Continuing education opens doors to career advancement, offering specialized positions, higher salaries, and leadership roles. With the benefit of classroom experience and focused continuing education, teachers are well-suited to become excellent educational leaders who can drive change and innovation within schools, ensuring they effectively meet evolving educational demands.4

## How Continuing Education Can Help Improve Student Outcomes

Modern teaching is an inclusive, technology-enabled, and highly interactive endeavor supported by research. It focuses on helping students from diverse backgrounds build cognitive and social skills that serve them well beyond graduation. Continuing professional development is essential for teachers to remain effective in this dynamic context, helping them with integrating technology into the classroom and adapting to evolving educational needs.

### Evidence-Based and Innovative Practices

Current practices are evidence-based, meaning they are proven strategies developed through research and tested for positive educational outcomes across diverse student groups. Innovative, evidence-based practices, many of which rely on [technology integration](https://onlineprograms.education.uiowa.edu/blog/integrating-technology-in-the-classroom-best-practices), include blended learning, flipped classrooms, and project-based learning.

### Culturally Responsive and Differentiated Instruction

Differentiated instruction and culturally responsive instruction are related strategies that aim to unlock the learning potential of diverse students. Professional education helps teachers understand and effectively implement these strategies.

Differentiated instruction uses a data-informed approach to create small groups tailored to students' unique learning needs, allowing teachers to provide personalized support.7

Culturally responsive instruction empowers students by acknowledging the contributions of all students, connecting classroom content to real-world experiences and using cultural references to impart knowledge. This approach promotes positive social interactions through diverse group work, addresses social barriers and promotes equity.8

### Enhancing Learning With Formative Assessments

Formative assessments enhance student learning by providing real-time feedback that guides instructional practices. In K-12 contexts, these assessments offer insights into student progress, allowing educators to adjust teaching methods to meet learning goals.9 In professional development settings, formative assessments help teachers identify areas for growth, ensuring continuous improvement in their teaching practice. Administered through quick quizzes or feedback sessions, these assessments focus on learning processes rather than final grades, fostering a culture of growth and development.

## Elevate Your Education Career With Professional Development at the University of Iowa Online

Continuing professional development ensures teachers understand and can use the latest strategies and tools to navigate and thrive in today’s dynamic educational landscape. Continuing education helps you adapt to and apply new education technologies, as well as understand and implement the latest in teaching strategy and techniques. Whether you want to serve students as a classroom teacher or through educational leadership roles, staying current helps you meet diverse learners' needs and enhance overall educational success.

The [Iowa Online Master of Arts in Teaching, Leadership, and Cultural Competency (MATLCC)](https://onlineprograms.education.uiowa.edu/master-of-arts-in-teaching-leadership-and-cultural-competency) program incorporates the elements of effective professional development derived from research, offering a comprehensive learning experience. Core [coursework](https://onlineprograms.education.uiowa.edu/master-of-arts-in-teaching-leadership-and-cultural-competency/curriculum) aligns with the National Board for Professional Teaching Standards, ensuring you're well-prepared for the NBPTS Critical Reflection certification. Customize your degree with electives tailored to your career goals in this flexible, powerful, and affordable program designed by teachers for teachers.

Contact an [admissions outreach advisor](https://uofiowa.myadvisorappt.com/#/) today to learn more about how Iowa's Online MATLCC program can advance your career and help you make a meaningful impact in education.

Return to [University of Iowa Education Blog](https://onlineprograms.education.uiowa.edu/blog)

# The Three Most Common Types of Teacher Professional Development and How to Make Them Better

November 15, 2023

**Topics:** 
[Professional Development](https://www.powerschool.com/resources/?filter_resource_types=post&filter_topics=professional-development)

## 

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%201080'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-1-110823.png)
![](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-1-110823.png)

Professional development for educators is inextricably linked to student outcomes. Yet, PowerSchool’s [2023 Education Focus Report](https://www.powerschool.com/edtech-focus-report-2023/) found that only 4 in 10 educators say they receive quality professional development to personalize learning for students. At a time when students who were in school during the pandemic are being called “[education’s lost generation](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/covids-education-crisis-a-lost-generation/),” improving teacher professional development is urgently essential.

## **What is Professional Development** **for Teachers?**

Professional development for teachers refers to an educator’s formal ongoing career growth, which consists of improving their knowledge, skills, and competencies to support their effectiveness in the classroom. [Continuous professional learning](https://www.nea.org/professional-excellence/professional-learning/teachers) throughout a teacher’s career includes staying current with effective best practices; addressing the changing landscape of the needs of students, families, and communities; being informed about policies and statutes, and any changes to those policies and statutes, that govern the work of educators; and adapting to evolving professional responsibilities, such as increasing the use of technology tools in the classroom.

![Image shows 5 ways professional development improves teaching: 1. Teachers learn to teach better, 2. Teachers feel supported, 3. Teachers increase their competence, 4. Teachers model being students, and 5, Teacher collaboration.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%201080'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Image shows 5 ways professional development improves teaching: 1. Teachers learn to teach better, 2. Teachers feel supported, 3. Teachers increase their competence, 4. Teachers model being students, and 5, Teacher collaboration.](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-2-110623.png)

![Image shows 5 ways professional development improves teaching: 1. Teachers learn to teach better, 2. Teachers feel supported, 3. Teachers increase their competence, 4. Teachers model being students, and 5, Teacher collaboration.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%201080'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Image shows 5 ways professional development improves teaching: 1. Teachers learn to teach better, 2. Teachers feel supported, 3. Teachers increase their competence, 4. Teachers model being students, and 5, Teacher collaboration.](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-2-110623.png)

## **5 Ways Professional Development Improves Teaching**

Professional development [helps educators grow their knowledge, skills, and practice in these five essential ways](https://online.queens.edu/resources/article/professional-development-for-educators/):

![Image featuring 3 types of teacher professional development: Periodic workshops, in-class observation, and single-session seminars. ](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20793%20333'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Image featuring 3 types of teacher professional development: Periodic workshops, in-class observation, and single-session seminars. ](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/three-most-common-types-of-teacher-professional-development-blog-body-graphic-041322.jpeg)

![Image featuring 3 types of teacher professional development: Periodic workshops, in-class observation, and single-session seminars. ](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20793%20333'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Image featuring 3 types of teacher professional development: Periodic workshops, in-class observation, and single-session seminars. ](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/three-most-common-types-of-teacher-professional-development-blog-body-graphic-041322.jpeg)

## **Three Most Common Types of Teacher Professional Development**

The top three types of teacher professional development are periodic workshops, in-class observation, and single-session seminars. By keeping a few general principles in mind, you can improve each type of [teacher PD](https://www.powerschool.com/resources/blog/a-teacher-professional-development-overview/).

### **Periodic Workshops**

Many consulting companies offer workshops series. They contract with a school or district for a year—or perhaps longer—to deliver regular professional development around a certain theme, such as “Creating Common Core Assessments” or “Explicit Direct Instruction Lesson Design Training.” The workshops can cover topics from academic content to school culture to DEIJ initiatives.  

An alternative to bringing in an outside consultant is to invite teachers to present at periodic workshops that are centered on themes such as “Innovative Assessments” or “Project-Based Learning Curriculum.” This provides teachers the opportunity to share with their colleagues what they’re doing with their students, moving their work beyond the individual classroom and into the school community at large.

### **In-class Observation**

While in-class observation is typically thought of as a component of teacher evaluations, it doesn’t always have to be so formal. Consider instituting an “open door” policy for your teachers. When a teacher has a free period, they are welcome to drop in on a class with an open door to observe the teacher and students at work. Not only does this help build a culture of collegiality among your teachers, but it also encourages ongoing conversations about best practices, content knowledge, and classroom management strategies.

### **Single-session seminars**

Single-session seminars are a “one-off” form of PD that typically takes place on a day the students have off from school. Often seen as ineffective or a burdensome obligation among teachers, these sessions may lack efficacy because the subject matter is irrelevant or abstract, and it takes time away from the many other tasks teachers already don’t have the time to complete.  

The best way to improve single-session seminars is to ask your teachers how they want to spend the time. Gathering in [professional learning communities (PLCs)](https://www.powerschool.com/blog/plc-handbook/)? Working on a collective inquiry? Discussing school policies such as discipline or summer reading? When teachers have a say in how they spend their PD time, they’re more invested and engaged, and more likely to feel it was beneficial to their work.  

![teacher shortage survival guide ebook cover](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20612%20413'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![teacher shortage survival guide ebook cover](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/02/teacher-shortage-survival-ebook_thumbnail-022423-612x413.jpeg)

### Learn More About Personalized Professional Development

## **What is the most effective professional development for teachers?**

There is increasing evidence that [instructional coaching and teacher collaboration are the most effective forms](https://www.edweek.org/leadership/what-works-and-what-doesnt-in-teacher-pd/2022/10#:~:text=Coaching%20and%20teacher%20collaboration%20are%20key%20strategies&text=Coaching%E2%80%94which%20can%20include%20modeling,instructional%20quality%20and%20student%20outcomes.) of professional development for teachers. The biggest benefit of instructional coaching is that it is specifically tailored to the individual growth needs of the teacher. The one-on-one format provides targeted support and ongoing and embedded feedback. For example, if a teacher particularly struggles with classroom management, their instructional coach will work with them to establish order, maximize instructional time, maintain a safe and respectful learning environment, and promote student engagement in learning. At the same time, that teacher’s colleague may be an expert at classroom management, but struggles to implement technology tools in their work. Their instructional coach will help them integrate edtech tools into their curriculum.  

Teacher collaboration is highly beneficial for professional development because it: 

![Image featuring 7 key elements of Effective PD: focus on content, active learning, work-embedded collaboration, model best practices, coach and support, incorporate feedback and reflective practice, and deliver the PD over time.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%201080'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Image featuring 7 key elements of Effective PD: focus on content, active learning, work-embedded collaboration, model best practices, coach and support, incorporate feedback and reflective practice, and deliver the PD over time.](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-3-110623.png)

![Image featuring 7 key elements of Effective PD: focus on content, active learning, work-embedded collaboration, model best practices, coach and support, incorporate feedback and reflective practice, and deliver the PD over time.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%201080'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Image featuring 7 key elements of Effective PD: focus on content, active learning, work-embedded collaboration, model best practices, coach and support, incorporate feedback and reflective practice, and deliver the PD over time.](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/pd-for-teachers-powerpage-graphic-3-110623.png)

## **7** **Key Elements of Effective PD**

In a report focused on the features of effective professional development and its impact on student outcomes, Darling-Hammond, Hyler, and Gardner identified several [key elements of effective teacher PD](https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/effective-teacher-professional-development-brief): 

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20612%20420'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://www.powerschool.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/usa-silhouette-612x420.png)

### Download [this case study](https://www.powerschool.com/case-studies/improving-teacher-evaluations-with-powerschool-unified-talent-perform/) to see how one district helped teachers grow with quality, consistent feedback.

## **3 Ways to Improve Professional Development**

One of the most common challenges school administrators face is providing relevant and effective [professional development](https://www.nea.org/professional-excellence/professional-learning/teachers) (PD). The most successful professional learning opportunities are meaningful, can be implemented and sustained, and lead to enhanced student learning.

### **1. How to Improve Periodic Workshops**

There is often a disconnect between introducing a new idea, implementing that idea, and sustaining that implementation. That’s why it’s important to move beyond periodic workshops to ongoing PD that provides opportunities to apply and refine new ideas over time.  

[The Center for Public Education reported](https://www.teachthought.com/pedagogy/5-strategies-better-teacher-professional-development/) that allotting a significant amount of time—say, as much as 50 hours—gives teachers the chance to demonstrate mastery of a new instructional strategy. Teachers should be active, not passive, during those 50 hours. 

Which strategy do you think is more effective: reviewing a PowerPoint and reading examples about integrating technology into lesson plans, or actively building a Webquest with a future lesson in mind, peer-reviewing the results, sharing the work with students, and getting back together to reflect on learning outcomes? 

You guessed it. Ongoing, active, and continuou

# Top Emerging Trends for K-12 Professional Development in 2024

##### Share article

![](https://www.frontlineeducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/PG-Infographic-Blog_feat_Graphic.png)

Professional development (PD) in K-12 education is undergoing a significant transformation. Districts across the nation are adopting innovative strategies to boost teacher engagement and retention. Our recent [K-12 Lens Survey](https://www.frontlineeducation.com/k-12-lens-a-survey-report-from-frontline-education/), which collected insights from over 700 administrators, reveals emerging 2024 trends and actionable opportunities in professional learning. These findings highlight the importance of personalization, effective methods like mentoring, and the need to address instructional shifts to create more engaging and impactful PD experiences.

### Key Professional Learning Priorities

Districts are focusing on several shifts in professional learning:

![](https://www.frontlineeducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Key-Professional-Learning-Priorities.svg)

Despite these priorities, only **42%** of respondents reported that their **PD systems can facilitate personalized professional learning based on evaluation results** and teacher goals. This gap highlights a significant opportunity for districts to align evaluation and professional learning systems to enhance personalization and engagement.

Nearly **80%** of districts believe that **coaching and mentoring** are the most effective PD offerings for supporting teacher retention. Additionally, coaching and professional learning communities are seen as the most engaging forms of professional development.

**PD Offerings that Boosts Retention**

![](https://www.frontlineeducation.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/K12_Lens_Figure_5.svg)

To address these challenges and improve teacher engagement and retention, districts should consider:

# PDMOST Professional Development Models and Outcomes for Science Teachers

![PDMOST Professional Development Models and Outcomes for Science Teachers](pdmost_main/images/00_desktop_banner_w925_h85.png)
![decorative icon](pdmost_main/images/02_desktop_trends_icon_66w_58h.png)

# Trends in Professional Development

There is near universal agreement that a common purpose of Professional Development is to affect change in educators, their classrooms, and students. Recently, changes in how PD is planned, assessed, and evaluated has led to more robust professional learning opportunities and important trends in PD offerings. This revised structure of professional learning is improving the impacts that PD has on teacher practices and student learning outcomes.

# Trends in Professional Development

There is near universal agreement that a common purpose of Professional Development is to affect change in educators, their classrooms, and students. Recently, changes in how PD is planned, assessed, and evaluated has led to more robust professional learning opportunities and important trends in PD offerings. This revised structure of professional learning is improving the impacts that PD has on teacher practices and student learning outcomes.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that [supports HTML5 video](https://videojs.com/html5-video-support/)

## TRENDS AND THE CURRENT STATE OF PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT

Teacher professional development is a major focus of many systemic reform initiatives in education. Studies have shown that teacher success can be fostered through high-quality professional development that is sustained, connected to practice and school initiatives, focused on academic content, and supportive of strong working relationships among teachers. Three major goals of professional development include change in classroom practices of teachers, change in the attitudes and beliefs of teachers, and change in the learning outcomes of students (Guskey, 2002).

While there is a lack of rigorous studies and evidence of the impact that professional development has on student learning outcomes, there is evidence that focusing on subject matter knowledge (SMK) and pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) in PD programs is effective at positively influencing student learning. In fact, pedagogical content knowledge, including the knowledge of students’ misconceptions, is often thought to have the greatest effect on student learning.

We are starting to see more empirical evidence of the value of specific PD features. We are also seeing that the content of teachers’ professional development includes targeting improvements to content knowledge, pedagogical strategies, alignment of curriculum and assessment, and a range of other topics. More PD is being structured as “collective participation” so that teachers are participating alongside colleagues from their school and district with opportunities for ongoing collaboration (Garet, et al., 2001).

Professional learning programs are incorporating extended learning opportunities and sustained follow-up after a main PD event. Programs that have 30 or more hours of well-organized, carefully structured, purposefully directed, and SMK- and PCK-focus tend to be more effective over this time. However, if the time is not wisely used, we see no differences in the learning outcomes of students. Extended PD opportunities provide Professional Learning Communities and school-wide or district-wide teacher teams collaborative learning environments and ongoing support for professional learning, allowing for gradual integration of new teaching strategies and implementation of reformed learning strategies into classrooms.

### ONLINE VS. IN-PERSON PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT

Given the extent of websites and programs, it can be overwhelming and difficult to make sense of available Professional Development opportunities taking place across the country. Increasingly, online can offer a good choice, however, in-person can often be a better fit. The three types of online PD to consider are Synchronous (in real time, similar to a webinar or distance education course, virtual in-person setting); Asynchronous (self-paced, MOOC-like, discussion boards, etc.); and Hybrid (part of a larger PD, in-person with virtual collaboration between sessions). No matter which type of PD, one must consider his or her own learning goals and styles, but ultimately decide if online learning provides a benefit over in-person PD.

Bates, M., Phalen, L. & Moran, C. (2016). Online professional development: a primer. The Phi Delta Kappan, 97(5), 70-73. Retrieved from [http://www.jstor.org/stable/24579782](http://www.jstor.org/stable/24579782 "Online professional development: a primer.").

enriched_points.expanded_content: Teacher professional development includes workshops, online courses, peer collaboration, and conferences, offering diverse formats to suit different learning preferences and schedules.
Common practices include periodic workshops, in-class observations, and single-session seminars, which aim to enhance teaching effectiveness and foster collaborative learning among educators.

Engagement levels among teachers in professional development vary; while some find value, many express dissatisfaction, highlighting the need for more relevant and personalized learning opportunities.

Effective professional development positively impacts teaching quality by enhancing educators' skills, confidence, and instructional strategies, ultimately leading to improved student learning outcomes.
